perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall take responsible steps to ensure that all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party is kept confidential; provided, however, that such information may be disclosed to those employees or agents of the Recipient who have a need to know such information, or to such other party as may be agreed to by the Disclosing Party in writing, only if each such employee or party is informed by the Recipient of the confidential and proprietary nature of such information and of the confidentiality undertakings of the Recipient contained herein.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient should endeavor to take steps to keep Confidential Information confidential; provided, however, that such information may be disclosed to employees or agents of the Recipient who might benefit from knowing such information, or to such other party as may be seen fit by the Recipient, only if each such employee or party is generally aware of the confidential nature of such information.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'shall take responsible steps to ensure' with 'should endeavor to take steps,' weakening the obligation. 'Need to know' is replaced with 'might benefit from knowing' broadening the disclosure scope. 'Informed by the Recipient of the confidential and proprietary nature of such information' is replaced with 'generally aware of the confidential nature of such information' which weakens the requirement to actively inform them. All of these changes make the obligation ambiguous and harder to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which the original text's 'responsible steps to ensure' likely met. The modified text's 'endeavor to take steps' and 'generally aware' introduces ambiguity that weakens the protective measures, potentially falling short of the reasonable efforts standard required to maintain trade secret status under PUTSA. If the recipient does not take appropriate steps to keep a trade secret confidential, the discloser might lose protections from the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2022) - Damages :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5304 - Damages Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5304. Damages. (a) Monetary damages. --Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) Exemplary damages. --If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandated responsible steps to ensure confidentiality, whereas the modified language weakens this obligation to merely endeavoring to take steps. The LLM explanation and the modification explanation both highlight this weakening as potentially falling short of the reasonable efforts standard required to maintain trade secret status. The scraped law snippet from Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 discusses damages for misappropriation, indicating the importance of protecting trade secrets. The original text was more likely to meet the standard of protecting trade secrets; the changed text creates an ambiguous legal obligation which may cause the discloser to lose protections from the law, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: As used herein, "reasonable steps" means the steps that the Recipient takes to protect its own, similar confidential and proprietary information, which shall not be less than a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, "reasonable steps" may mean the steps that the Recipient subjectively believes are adequate to protect its own information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines 'reasonable steps' objectively, tying it to a 'reasonable standard of care.' The modified text makes it subjective ('Recipient subjectively believes are adequate'), which provides no real standard and would be difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA's requirement of 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy is undermined by the subjective definition in the modified text. What the recipient 'subjectively believes' may fall far short of what a court would consider reasonable, thus jeopardizing the trade secret status and conflicting with the intent of PUTSA.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the definition of 'reasonable steps' from an objective standard ('reasonable standard of care') to a subjective belief ('Recipient subjectively believes are adequate'). This undermines the legal obligation to take reasonable steps to protect confidential information because what a recipient 'subjectively believes' is adequate might not meet the legal standard of reasonableness, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection as indicated by the law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall promptly return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party in whatever medium, including any and all copies thereof. Such return (or, if authorized, destruction of Confidential Information) shall be confirmed in writing by the Recipient to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient should endeavor to return Confidential Information in its possession, provided it is reasonably accessible. A written confirmation of return is at the Recipient's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a firm obligation to promptly return all confidential information and confirm the return in writing. The modified text weakens this by changing 'shall promptly return' to 'should endeavor to return,' adding the condition 'provided it is reasonably accessible,' and making written confirmation discretionary. This provides loopholes for non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: While PUTSA doesn't explicitly mandate return of information in a non-disclosure agreement, the refusal to return information could be construed as continued misappropriation if the recipient uses or discloses the information after being requested to return it. The weakening of the return obligation and the elimination of mandatory written confirmation make it more difficult for the disclosing party to prove misappropriation, hindering their ability to seek injunctive relief under PUTSA if the recipient continues to use the information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5302 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation,
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to return confidential information and removes the mandatory written confirmation. The original text states the recipient 'shall promptly return' all confidential information and that this return 'shall be confirmed in writing'. The changed text softens this to 'should endeavor to return,' adds a condition of reasonable accessibility, and makes written confirmation discretionary. This undermines the disclosing party's ability to prove misappropriation if the recipient uses or discloses the information, as discussed in the Law Explanation. Snippet 2 defines 'misappropriation' as including 'acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means' or 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By weakening the obligation to return the information and eliminating mandatory written confirmation, the changed text makes it harder to prove that the recipient's continued possession or use of the information constitutes misappropriation, creating a loophole for non-compliance and hindering the disclosing party's ability to seek relief.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it would be impossible or inadequate to measure and calculate the other party's damages from any breach of the covenants set forth in this Agreement. Accordingly, the parties agree that if either party breaches or threatens to breach any of such covenants, the non-breaching party will have available, in addition to any other right or remedy available, the right to obtain an injunction from a court of competent jurisdiction restraining such breach or threatened breach and to specific performance of any such provision of this Agreement. The parties further agree that no bond or other security shall be required in obtaining such equitable relief and each party hereby consents to the issuance of such injunction and to the ordering of specific performance.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that determining damages from a breach might be difficult. Therefore, the parties agree that in the event of a breach, the non-breaching party may seek equitable relief, such as an injunction, at the court's discretion. Whether a bond is required for such relief will be determined by the court.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a strong statement agreeing that damages would be impossible to calculate and consenting to injunctive relief without a bond. The modified text weakens this by stating that determining damages 'might be difficult', replacing 'will have the right to obtain an injunction' with 'may seek equitable relief...at the court's discretion,' and stating that whether a bond is required 'will be determined by the court.' This introduces uncertainty about the availability of equitable relief.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 1531
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania Rule of Civil Procedure 1531 governs injunctions. While the rule gives the court discretion on whether to require a bond, the original agreement expresses consent that no bond is required. The modified text removes this consent, giving the court full discretion and potentially requiring a bond that the parties initially agreed would not be necessary. This creates ambiguity about the terms of obtaining an injunction, and conflicts with the parties' initial agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text expresses a clear agreement between parties regarding injunctive relief and waiving the bond requirement. The changed text introduces ambiguity by giving the court discretion on whether to grant equitable relief and whether a bond is required. The Law Explanation states that the original agreement expresses consent that no bond is required. The modified text removes this consent, giving the court full discretion and potentially requiring a bond that the parties initially agreed would not be necessary. This creates ambiguity about the terms of obtaining an injunction, and conflicts with the parties' initial agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, while not fully decipherable due to formatting issues, appears to relate to court procedures, which aligns with the context of injunctions and bond requirements. The weakening of the original agreement and the introduction of court discretion contradict the initial intent and agreement of the parties, creating a legal contradiction as defined by the Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation Perturbation Type.
